dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to [CITY] dialysis clinic for a transfer. Patient is a 71-year-old female with chest pain, confusion, and hyperglycemia.
Hello, I'm Alex with the rescue squad. Can I ask the staff what the main issue is with the patient today?
Our call type is a transfer for chest pain, hyperglycemia, and confusion. I'll need more information from the staff so we can properly prepare for transport.
She came in with generalized weakness today and had chest pain. She vomited during dialysis and became increasingly confused and disoriented.
Prior to dialysis, she was noted to have a heart rate of 120. She only lasted about an hour and a half of the scheduled four-hour treatment.
Ma'am, can you give me your name?
Katherine.
Katherine, what day is today?
I think it's… Thursday.
Katherine, can you raise your left arm for me?
Hmm… okay.
She is maintaining her airway. No audible or visible signs of airway obstruction.
Her breathing appears even with no signs of distress.
Her respirations are 20 per minute. Equal bilateral chest rise and fall.
Pulse is strong and fast. Her blood pressure is elevated systolic and low diastolic. Skin is warm, dry, and without cyanosis.
Her respiratory rate is 20 per minute, symmetrical with no accessory muscle use.
Pulse is 131 and regular.
Blood pressure is 152 over 63.
Oxygen saturation is at 100% on room air.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia on screen.
Katherine, when did you first start to feel unwell?
She started dialysis but only lasted about half of the session when she began feeling worse. She vomited, and after that, she became very confused.
Her medical history includes end-stage renal disease, insulin-dependent diabetes, and a prior stroke. No known drug allergies.
She is confused, weak, and having trouble with time and place orientation.
She likely had a light intake prior to dialysis, but can't confirm what specifically. She did vomit once during treatment.
She is taking Lantus, Humalog, Lipitor, and several other medications for her diabetes and heart condition.
No drug allergies are listed on her medication sheet.
Katherine, are you experiencing any pain?
I don't think so.
Her skin is warm and dry with slight poor turgor and tenting noted. No bruising, rashes, or wounds.
No signs of nuchal rigidity, bulging neck veins, or abnormal eye movements. No lymphadenopathy noted.
No drainage from ears or nose, mouth dry, no facial droop. Airway patent.
Sinus tach on EKG and confusion are concerning. We are following the chest pain-cardiac suspected protocol.
Both our and facility glucometers show high readings-can't determine exact levels.
The dialysis staff noted the patient is afebrile at 98.6°F.
Four-lead ECG placed and a 12-lead EKG was performed, showing sinus tach with possible right and left atrial enlargement.
Her pulse has settled to 125 and regular rhythm.
Blood pressure now reads 137 over 75, slightly decreased diastolic.
Oxygen saturation is now at 99% on room air.
Second 12-lead performed confirming sinus tach-no new findings.
We've positioned her in a position of comfort. We'll use a three-person drag for safe transfer to the stretcher.
Her hospital gown has been removed and body is covered for warmth and modesty.
[COMPANY_NAME] is aware. We are transporting as directed for hyperglycemia and confusion.
The ED has been briefed. She needs immediate assessment for confusion, hyperglycemia, and tachycardia. Handing off now.
